Bank of Canada surprises with 100-basis point rate hike as price risks gather steam   OTTAWA — The Bank of Canada on Wednesday raised its benchmark interest rate by a full percentage point, surprising markets with its biggest rake hike since 1998 in a bid to tame soaring inflation.

AUArts welcomes new dean of academic programs   Janis Goldie moved to Calgary from Sudbury, Ont., to accept her appointment as the new dean of academic programs at Alberta University of the Arts (AUArts). But she is certainly no stranger to this city, having earned both her masters and her PhD in communications and media studies at the University of Calgary.

Opinion: Why Canadians should care about yet another constitutional court battle   It feels like constitutional wrangling is the new national pastime. Last year, it was carbon pricing; this year Bill C-69 is back.

Ford: My Stampede love affair all began with a lightning strike of luck on Kids' Day   Calgarians love the Stampede. Not all of us, of course. But those are the outliers; the ones who escape to the mountains; ones who think all this cowboy kitsch is so down-market they sneer at the thought.

Your letters for July 12, 2022   Councillor needs to answer Re: Police investigation urged in Carra integrity breach, July 7 Having shouted from atop his moral high ground for Counc. Chu to resign when a decades-old scandal of his was brought to light, I should hope Carra follows his own advice and resigns from his position on council. I also hope police and provincial authorities properly investigate the latest allegations regarding this undisclosed land purchase and his role in the easing of building restrictions for that land. Added to his being investigated for an alleged assault in Bridgeland and being sanctioned for online bullying, it is my belief Counc. Carra is no longer fit to serve Calgarians. Brian Findlay, Calgary

Opinion: A growing number of Calgary companies are becoming tech trailblazers   As Calgary celebrates its past during the 10 days of Stampede, a walk through the downtown core provides a window into the city’s future. Across the street from the Fairmont Palliser hotel stands the old headquarters of PanCanadian, which was the largest producer of conventional crude oil in Canada and one of the biggest natural gas producers during its heyday. Today, the revamped office tower is buzzing with activity from a sector whose importance to the city is growing by the day: tech.

How a teen 'vampire's' blog blew up a murder trial — and prevented a wrongful conviction   The blog contained a description of her 'likes,' which included: 'Blood, pain ... rain, darkness, thunder ... fire, my bedroom, sex ... cemeteries, knives'
True Crime Byline: We should have seen JoAnn Wilson's murder coming   Leader-Post city editor Barb Pacholik recalls the many turns of the Colin Thatcher case across decades.

True Crime Byline: How one childhood incident pushed Dawn Crey into a downward spiral from which she would never escape   The new podcast True Crime Byline looks back at The Vancouver Sun investigation into Vancouver's missing women, a case that eventually led to the arrest and conviction of Robert Pickton. This profile of missing woman Dawn Crey was part of a series of investigative stories published in 2001.

True Crime Byline: A Politician Murdered his Ex Wife. We Should Have Seen it Coming   Looking back on the murder of JoAnn Wilson today, what's most surprising is that we were surprised it happened at all.

True Crime Byline podcast: The Article that Blew Up a Murder Trial   Listen to this podcast episode for the shocking story of how a National Post article about a teen vampire led to a mistrial, as told by reporter Joseph Brean

Former Calgary Opera artistic director Bramwell Tovey dies   PROVIDENCE, R.I. — Bramwell Tovey, a Grammy and Juno Award-winning composer and conductor who was artistic director of Calgary Opera for a year and a half, has died.
Theatre Calgary's summer Shakespeare show remains on the go   Once again, Theatre Calgary’s Shakespeare by the Bow will hit the road and become Shakespeare on the Go.

Quaintly named Kat and Dave Show brings superstar hits to Alberta shows   During the early months of the pandemic, Canadian-born music icon David Foster and his wife Katharine McPhee created a little show for Instagram called The Kat & Dave Show. This year, they took that little show out of their living room, beefed it up considerably and are taking it on the road.

Review: Bell Grandstand Show rivals the nightly fireworks for sound and colour   The 2022 Bell Grandstand Show is an explosion of lights, colour, sounds and movement, fittingly capped off with a spectacular fireworks display. In his song Bring Down the House, Brody sings about being that simple country boy who drives a truck but also has money in his pocket, a beautiful girl on his arm and the ability to bring down the house, which he certainly does. The Grandstand stage was made for his powerhouse talents. Amplified’s second headliner, award-winning trick roper Loop Rawlins, arrived on the back of a silver pickup truck and gave his ropes a good workout. He was joined by the Young Canadians who took up lassos and joined him for a most impressive finale to their number. The show’s final headliner, Canadian Indigenous singer Shawnee Kish gave Amplified a true adrenaline fix with her hit song I Got It Bad and then really amped things up with a meteoric rendition of Saturday Night. Kish returned for Amplified’s big finale where she sang and moved through fountains of water and mini towers of flames, eventually joined by the entire cast minus Dean Brody.

Brooks: Deerfoot City hosts PARK’s Block Party   PARK, which stands for Promoting Artists/Redefining Kulture (PARK), creates opportunities for developing fashion designers, artists, small businesses and creatives across Canada by raising awareness. Readers may recall innovative PARK events over the years at venues as varied as Studio Bell, Spruce Meadows, Chinook Centre Parkade, Brookfield Place and the George C. King Bridge in East Village, to name a few.

Style Therapy: From rebels to rockers, denim suits us all   It’s safe to say we all love jeans. They have influenced culture and style in the last two centuries probably more than we realize. Jeans have been worn by miners, cowboys, Hollywood stars, rebels, rockers and influencers. Almost all fashion designers and luxury labels have featured jeans in their collections.

As Told To: 'Not everyone knows how to react to someone with a life-altering injury'   But you can start by not raising your voice or feeling the need to speak slower, says Russ Davies, who has been living with a brain injury since 2009: 'People think you aren't capable of having a conversation.'

What are the signs of cataracts?   While there is currently no known way to prevent cataracts, you can reduce your risk by wearing sunglasses, eating foods rich in antioxidants, and getting regular eye exams.

Drinking alone when you're young linked to alcohol problems later in life   Researchers say young people often drink alone as a way to cope with negative emotions, a pattern of use that has been linked to alcohol use disorder.

Arthritis is so much more than just a few aches and pains   Since being diagnosed with idiopathic arthritis, Rachel Gehue has had fusions on both of her wrists, and her foot, as well as splints made for the fingers on both hands.
